And so it will be additive.
It's my take.

Some of the things we do today will get commoditized, but it be very additive on the new things we start to do.
Yeah, that's been my experience too.

mean, that's obviously nothing is strictly tied to bottom line, but like even less so, right, where you have this overabundance.

But I am so curious about this from like the tooling and use of the stuff is great, but is there, do you see this dwindling thing where we're losing to LLM-based traffic and like traditional search traffic and traditional distribution discovery methods are going away?
Like, can we supplant that?
Is AI an answer to supplant that?
Or how do we think about a world where your customer journey is so much different than it has been traditionally?
Yeah, I have a lot of thoughts on this topic.

Like, I think one of the things I think about is that over the last, basically since the dawn of the internet, the dawn of the internet started changing the information asymmetry between buyers and sellers, right?
It used to, buyer used to have, or the seller used to have all the information.
And by the time we were to like 2015, it's like, oh, the buyer has the majority of that information.
Now I think.
We're just full information symmetry, right?
No asymmetry, which I think is kind of wild.
And I think that's going to just mean that businesses have to be way more transparent.
And what is internal is external.
And there's some implications to that.
But more so, what we're seeing is that an LLM's ability to contextualize products and recommendations or strategies is so personal that the consideration and intent of those people is much higher.

Like in early data, we're seeing like if you're coming from an LLM-based type of search query versus a traditional-based kind of search query, you are converting into a customer twice as fast and at like 12x the rate, not like 1 or 2x, like over an order of magnitude more, which is great.
The problem is there's a lot less people and there's volume game.
And so I think if you're a marketer, you're a seller or a CEO.
Yeah.
One of the challenges is like visits and revenue are going to get decoupled for the first time.
Yes.
And that's going to be like a very hard thing to understand because it used to be your conversion rates were pretty static, but your visits was very movable.
So if get more visits, I will get more customers.
And now it's kind of the inverse is true.
It can really help your conversion rates improve and change dramatically, but it's much harder to move that visits number because you have established channels like Google going away.
That being said, there are influencers, there's Substack, there's Reddit.
We're getting to this unbundling where there are going to be new channels, right?
We're just in this sticky middle ground of the old channels are going away and the new channels aren't very big yet or aren't here yet.
And so this is like a little extra painful time of that transition.
Yeah, it feels like you can't see the other side, but then when you articulate the other side, it actually makes a lot of sense, right?
Yeah, and it's one of those things that if you, it would break the entire logic of the history of the internet.
Yeah.
From a marketing perspective, to not have new opportunities come open.
But since AOL started, every change has opened massive new opportunities for marketers that weren't there before.
And to not think those are coming, I think is just wrong.
It's just, when are they coming?
How are they distributed?
Is I think what's causing most people the challenges they're facing.

a horrible way to consume information, right?

Like, I don't think most people actually have an appreciation for it.

data on that customer as its own profile of who it thinks the customer is.

did a podcast today with the new Cloud Connector and I had, it was an analyst.

built an entire lead nurturing campaign, it built an A-B testing model and framework, like everything in like 20 minutes.

I also wanted to talk, just change topic basically entirely, but like prompt engineering, know that's been a subject for you in talking about that, especially for marketers.

Do you think that that is one of these, like I seem to see a lot of sentiment that it's becoming less important as the models become better, like that there is less value in being able to prompt intelligently and like.
There's a flattening there at the skill level as the models are better at interpreting what it is you actually want.
Do you think that's the case, or do you think there's always going to be value in people who are very good at crafting how they speak to and interact with LLMs?
Look, I think we're 25 years or whatever into Google, and there's still expertise in writing Boolean search queries and those types.

I don't think it's that dissimilar, right, where it was very important for everything, you know, prompt engineering, like, in the early days, to get a good result for anything, you need to, like, know prompting.
And now, if you want some very specific or higher quality output, I do think you need to know.
The good thing is, is that these models are good engineers, right, and so you can have them work with you and work with them to engineer the prompt.
I think what most people are missing is, like, how do I get the data?
And then research and then craft the prompt before actually do the thing I'm trying to do.
Because once you have a really good, well-crafted prompt, the right context and research, then like that first shot response is very good.
Yes.
Right.
Yeah.
And it just takes a little bit of follow-up refinement to nudge it in the right direction.
But yeah, it is all those ingredient pieces.
And I think people are bad at identifying maybe the artifact pieces, like the component pieces that you want to feed into the machine to get the result you're looking Yeah.
What I found is if you get good at prompting and you, you know, you do some deep research, you use it like a connector, like HubSpot or something, and you get all the context in there.
Then like I can be in chat at QBT and just kind of open canvas, have this first pass as something that's pretty good.
And then I'll just do some manual edits and then maybe like highlight a couple sentences and have the LLM, you know, give it some creative direction and have it redo like bits and pieces.
And like you can get to a high quality output.
Yeah, for sure.

I think somewhat related, but a new narrative that I'm hearing a lot, and I think this just came up on the official OpenAI podcast most recently, was that there's a shift now into what are the skill sets people think that people should be building to be successful in an AI-powered future?
And the key word now that everybody keeps keying to is management.

People should have good management skills because in the near future, current state maybe, your main job with AI is going to be managing a team of AI agents collaboratively with human employees and human talent.
Like, do you feel like that is accurate?

Or do you think that's kind of like maybe short term and maybe there's something else after that that like, because I feel like in the same way that we really emphasize like very, very careful, elaborate product engineering, and then that became like, no, you should be like a smart critical thinker maybe.
Like the management one seems like maybe it's an interim step and the machines are going to progress such that there's something else after that.
Like, do you have any thoughts about what skills we should be fostering now for upskilling?
look, I'm probably less on the management track than others.
I think management has long been kind of overrated in just, even in human culture.
Management's important, but like professional managers will always get beat out by like skilled craftspeople most of the time and building something, right?
And I don't think it's that dissimilar in the post-AI world.
I think curiosity, creativity, ambition, like one of the things we talk a lot about in marketing specifically is like taste.
And taste is that like unique point of view and unique understanding of your audience, what they need, the experiences that they're not getting, right?
Like that is, like you were talking a little bit about...
Yeah,
earlier about the nichification of the world.
Lost with all that are shared experiences and monoculture.
And there's a lot of nostalgia for that.
And I think that if you're really creative and ambitious, you can actually bring some of those shared experiences back.
That's why you're seeing a resurgence in a lot of in-person events and things happening, especially in marketing and especially in B2B.
And so I think it's more important to be a very curious first principle thinker and get really good at clarifying problems and stating problems than it is being a good manager.
I think the management stuff will get solved by technology long term.
That's my guess.
So maybe I'm agreeing with you that maybe the management stuff is a little bit more of a short term intermediary thing.
Yeah, it does feel like that to me.
But I am curious then about like, on the subject of like, taste and that, like, how do you, how does someone go about acquiring?
Or like, how do you help someone to acquire that?
So it's, so it's interesting, right?
I've, I've, I've been asked this question a lot.
And I've thought about this question a lot.
And to me, taste is the overlap of understanding a person or group of people.
You can tell me if you agree with this or not.
And deeply, deeply understanding a subject.
And like, when you can bring those two things together, you have really good taste.
Like in the pandemic, I was like bored.
I was looking for something to do.
